input_path: data\papers\Mertens and Denil - 2025 - Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A laboratory‑scale gantry crane comprising a steel gantry frame, a horizontal trolley rail, a vertical lifting column, a crane boom and a carriage, together with a controller that delivers motion commands. The system operates in a confined lab environment and is engineered for precise, repeatable positioning tasks. Its limited size, well‑defined kinematics and straightforward degrees of freedom make it an ideal platform for demonstrating digital‑twin concepts and for hands‑on research and educational use.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The digital twin delivers services such as real‑time monitoring (continuous validation), optimal control trajectory execution via a kinematic model, historical data logging of system states and commands, data visualization dashboards, and support for control‑strategy evolution. These services are aimed at researchers and educators who use the twin to test fault‑detection algorithms, synchronization methods, and control optimization in a repeatable setting.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The twin implementation relies exclusively on freely available, commonly used software. CAD geometry and kinematic models are stored in standard formats and processed through open‑source tools. Simulation and control are performed using publicly available frameworks, while historical logging is handled by lightweight, open‑source database solutions. All components are open‑source to ensure reproducibility and extensibility.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The architecture follows a C4 container model. Containers include: (1) Physical Crane – the physical hardware with controller; (2) Control Application – a Python microservice orchestrating operations; (3) Trajectory Generator Service – a Python microservice generating trajectories and communicating via MQTT; (4) Database – a TimescaleDB (or PostgreSQL) instance for continuous logging; (5) MQTT Broker – mediating message exchange between services; (6) Logging Application – a Python component that continuously records crane state; (7) Continuous Validation Service – a Python component that continuously compares sensor measurements to the digital model; and (8) Human‑Machine Interface – a web dashboard presenting real‑time status. All components are containerized or virtualised, using container orchestration for scalability, and the Digital Twin orchestrates interactions via the MQTT broker, coordinating control flow, data persistence, and validation across the system.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin contains a behavioral simulation model of the gantry crane’s cart and hoist subsystems. The model ingests real‑time sensor streams published over the MQTT broker Eclipse Mosquitto and stores both historical and current state in a TimescaleDB time‑series database. The data management architecture links sensor readings, model state, and simulation outputs so that the twin can perform online prediction and visualization.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The crane is equipped with two stepper motors: the first drives the cart horizontally along the rail, and the second controls the vertical hoist movement. Actuation commands are transmitted via MQTT topics to the respective motor controllers. The motors are addressed individually, and current and speed limits are defined in the motor controller firmware (details not provided).
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensor payload includes a swing angle encoder mounted on the trolley to capture angular deviation, a cart position encoder to measure horizontal displacement, and a hoist level sensor that indicates vertical lift position. Each sensor publishes its reading over the Eclipse Mosquitto MQTT broker. A logging application aggregates these readings in real‑time and writes them into TimescaleDB. Specific sampling frequencies, transmission rates, and accuracy specifications are not provided in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: A Continuous Validation Service runs continuously, comparing real‑time sensor measurements with the simulation’s predicted state. It computes general distance metrics between measured and simulated values; if a threshold is exceeded, it notifies operators. The service thus provides ongoing model validation by ensuring the twin’s behavior stays within acceptable bounds.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The Physical Crane Interface—a Python program that connects to the crane’s controllers—collects telemetry and forwards it to the Logging Application. The Logging Application persists this state continuously into a TimescaleDB time‑series database. The interface can also publish telemetry to the MQTT broker so that other Digital Twin services (Trajectory Generator, Simulation, Continuous Validation) can subscribe. The stored data represents the crane’s operational state and is queried by simulation and validation services for comparison with real‑time measurements.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The Control Application sends motion commands to the Physical Crane Interface, which translates them into controller‑specific instructions and forwards them to the crane hardware. Feedback from the hardware is received by the Physical Crane Interface, passed to the Logging Application, and stored in TimescaleDB, allowing operators and validation services to confirm execution. The Digital Twin services (Trajectory Generator, Simulation, Continuous Validation) provide optimal trajectory or motion plans that the Control Application consumes and sends as commands to the crane.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: All Digital Twin components communicate via an MQTT broker that acts as the central message bus. This broker supports publish–subscribe interactions between the Trajectory Generator, Simulation, Continuous Validation, Logging, and Dashboard services. The document does not specify further details on network topology, bandwidth, latency, reliability, or security protocols beyond the use of MQTT.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: All Python components (Physical Crane Interface, Control Application, Logging Application, Trajectory Generator Service, Simulation Service, Continuous Validation Service, Dashboard) are bundled into a single Python module named gantrylib for deployment simplicity. For ease of deployment, the TimescaleDB, MQTT broker, and Grafana Dashboard are containerized with Docker and run locally on the operator’s environment (no explicit cloud or edge deployment is described). Computational resource requirements are not quantified in the document; scalability, reliability, and availability mechanisms are not mentioned beyond the inference that Docker containers provide isolation for the database, broker, and dashboard.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The Lab‑Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin is built within a GitHub‑hosted repository following an agile software engineering workflow. Its core logic is implemented in Python 3.9 and packaged into Docker containers that expose RESTful APIs and MQTT endpoints. Version control is handled by Git, and continuous integration pipelines automatically run on every push to the main branch. Incoming telemetry from the physical gantry, captured by a Raspberry Pi sensor suite, is published to an Eclipse Mosquitto broker and persisted in a TimescaleDB (PostgreSQL 13) instance for long‑term analysis. The twin’s evolution is managed through incremental updates to the Docker images, unified via semantic versioning of the tags, and validated by unit tests of the control algorithms, integration tests against mock MQTT topics, and regression checks against recorded time‑series data.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The digital twin delivers runtime visualization, optimal control of the gantry crane, and continuous validation of its performance. Historical data are logged in TimescaleDB and visualized with Grafana dashboards connected to the MQTT broker. Operators receive real‑time alerts on the dashboard when key metrics (e.g., payload velocity or acceleration) deviate beyond pre‑defined thresholds, enabling timely decision‑making.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The system adopts open‑source industry standards: MQTT for publish/subscribe communication, TimescaleDB (PostgreSQL) for time‑series storage, Docker containers for deployment portability, and RESTful HTTP interfaces for service access. No formal certification or additional interoperability standards beyond MQTT are reported.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be



Joachim Denil

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extracted characteristic accurately describes the physical gantry crane, its components (frame, trolley rail, etc.), controller, and operational context (confined lab, precise positioning). It correctly identifies the system as a lab-scale setup suitable for education and research. Supported by quotes: 'lab-scale gantry crane and its digital twin', 'physical crane and its controller', and 'used for future research or education on the topic of digital twins'. It is specific and avoids vague speculation, though it could slightly elaborate on the kinematics mentioned in the text.

The extraction correctly lists the services provided by the digital twin: continuous validation (real-time monitoring), optimal trajectory generation (control), historical data logging, and data visualization. It also mentions support for control-strategy evolution via the 'DarTwin' notation. The target users (researchers and educators) are correctly identified. This is highly detailed and strongly grounded in the text. Supported by quotes: 'services for optimal control, historical data logging, data visualization and continuous validation' and 'used as a demonstrator for... DarTwin, a notation for system evolution'.

The extraction correctly identifies the tools and technologies used: CAD models, kinematic models, open-source simulation and control frameworks (Python, Docker), and open-source databases (TimescaleDB). It emphasizes the open-source and freely available nature of the implementation to ensure reproducibility. This aligns perfectly with the 'Abstract' and 'Implementation' sections. Supported by quotes: 'openly available and only relies on other freely available and commonly used software', 'TimescaleDB database... optimized for time series data', and 'Grafana dashboard'.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source text mentions general data flow and 'continuous validation' but lacks specific technical details on synchronization frequencies, latency requirements, or temporal granularity needed for this characteristic.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. Although the text mentions a teaching extension with a 'ship digital twin', it does not describe the hierarchical structure, coordination mechanisms, or architectural multiplicity details required by the characteristic definition.

The extraction correctly identifies the C4 model and the main components but contains inaccuracies. It misses the 'Simulation Service' and 'Physical Crane Interface' described in the text. It incorrectly claims 'All components are containerized' (the source states Python apps are bundled in a module while Database/Broker/Dashboard are containerized) and adds unsupported speculation about 'scalability'.

The extraction correctly states 'Not in Document'. The document focuses on the internal components of the digital twin (e.g., Python applications, TimescaleDB, MQTT Broker) and the physical crane interface. It does not describe integration with external enterprise systems, other digital twins, or specific interoperability standards beyond the internal use of MQTT. The authors note they 'utilize a more or less ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework,' indicating a lack of standard external integration patterns.

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately identifies the database (TimescaleDB) and the existence of a simulation service. It correctly notes the use of the MQTT broker (Eclipse Mosquitto) and the logging application. However, the specific phrasing 'behavioral simulation model of the gantry crane’s cart and hoist subsystems' is an interpretation of the text's mention of a 'simulation of the crane and it’s controllers' and a 'kinematics model'. Additionally, the claim that the model 'ingests real-time sensor streams published over the MQTT broker' is an inference, as the text states the MQTT broker is for 'signaling between services' and the logging application handles the state, but does not explicitly describe the data flow from sensors to model via MQTT. Supported by quotes: 'Simulation Service: a service that implements a simulation of the crane and it’s controllers', 'Database: a TimescaleDB database', 'Logging Application: a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database'.

The extraction correctly identifies the two stepper motors and their functions (cart and hoist movement) based on the component diagram. However, the claim that 'Actuation commands are transmitted via MQTT topics to the respective motor controllers' is not supported by the source text. The text mentions the MQTT broker is for 'signaling between services' and the 'Physical Crane Interface' connects to the controllers, but does not explicitly state MQTT is used for motor commands. Furthermore, the details about 'current and speed limits' in the firmware are not found in the document. Supported by quotes: 'Two Motors and associated Controllers, one to control the cart’s movement, one to control the hoisting movement.', 'Cart Motor [Component: Stepper]', 'Hoist Motor [Component: Stepper]'.

The extraction lists a 'swing angle encoder' which is supported by the text. However, it also lists a 'cart position encoder' and a 'hoist level sensor' which are not explicitly mentioned as distinct physical sensing components in the provided source text. The text explicitly lists 'Swing Angle Encoder' and 'Anemometer' as measurement components, along with Motors and Controllers, but does not mention position encoders for the cart or hoist as separate sensors in the component list. The claim about publishing readings 'over the Eclipse Mosquitto MQTT broker' is also not explicitly supported; the text states the broker is for 'signaling between services'. Supported by quotes: 'Swing Angle Encoder measures the swinging motion of the hoist', 'Anemometer measures the wind speed'.

The extraction provides a highly accurate and detailed summary of the Continuous Validation Service described in the text. It correctly captures the function of comparing measurements with simulations, the use of distance metrics, the threshold checking mechanism, and the operator notification process. Supported by quotes: 'Continuous Validation Service: a service that continuously compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories', 'It calculates distance metrics between them and compares the value with an acceptable threshold', 'In case the threshold is breached operators are notified via the dashboard'.

block_4_docs_retrieved:
III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_


Figure 2 shows the context diagram of the system. In
essence, it shows the high-level abstraction of what a digital
twin is, and two actors that interact with the system. The
operator is the actor that would operate and monitor this
system day to day, whilst the researcher can perform digital
twin research on it.
Figure 3 shows the container [6] diagram of the system. We
have limited ourselves to the most necessary relationships to
keep the diagram readable.
The gantry crane system, which can operate without digital
twin if needed, consists of five containers.
**Physical Crane:** the actual hardware of the crane.


5 [https://c4model.com/](https://c4model.com/)
6 Not a Docker container, a container is an abstraction in the C4 model for
something that must be there for the system to work.


Fig. 3. Container diagram of the system.



**Physical Crane Interface:** the interface to the physical
crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the

crane.

**Control Application:** a Python application that uses the
crane interface to control the crane.

**Logging Application:** a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.
**Human Machine Interface:** a Python front-end for the
operator to configure and control the crane.
The digital twin consists of six containers. We’ll describe
them bottom up.

**Database:** a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data

created by the system and its various services.
**Dashboard:** a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data from

the database.

**Trajectory Generator Service:** a service that utilizes a
kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement
trajectories for the crane. Such optimal trajectories have no
residual swinging motion at the end.
**Simulation Service:** a service that implements a simulation
of the crane and it’s controllers. It can simulate for example
trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator
**Continuous Validation Service:** a service that continuously
compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics
between them and compares the value with an acceptable
threshold. In case the threshold is breached operators are
notified via the dashboard and can take appropriate action.
**MQTT Broker:** an MQTT broker for signaling between
services.

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give

block_4_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately identifies the Physical Crane Interface as a Python program and the data flow to the TimescaleDB via the Logging Application. It correctly lists the services involved. However, the claim that the interface publishes telemetry directly to the MQTT broker for service subscriptions is not explicitly stated in the text; the text only mentions MQTT is for 'signaling between services' and that the Logging Application handles the database persistence.

The description correctly identifies the flow from the Control Application to the Physical Crane Interface and the role of the Trajectory Generator. It accurately captures the feedback loop involving the Logging Application and database. It is specific to the crane system and aligns well with the architectural description provided.

The extraction correctly identifies the MQTT broker (Eclipse Mosquitto) as the communication method and accurately notes that the document does not provide further details on network topology or protocols. This precise handling of both present and absent information merits a high score.

The extraction correctly describes the bundling of Python components into the 'gantrylib' module, the containerization of the Database, MQTT Broker, and Dashboard with Docker, and the local deployment on a personal computer. However, it incorrectly lists the 'Dashboard' as one of the Python components bundled into 'gantrylib', whereas the text separates the Dashboard (Grafana) as a containerized component distinct from the Python module. Quotes: 'we bundled all the Python C4 Containers in a Python module named gantrylib.', 'For ease of deployment, the Database, MQTT Broker and Dashboard C4 Containers are containerized with Docker.', 'plug in the physical crane to a personal computer, and run all of the software on that computer.'

block_5_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.

The extraction contains detailed claims about specific versions (Python 3.9, PostgreSQL 13), CI pipelines, Raspberry Pi sensor suite, and specific testing types (unit, integration, regression). The note about no formal certification is also consistent with the text.
